expanded_description: Understanding how to write electron configurations for ions (cations and anions), including the rules for adding or removing electrons, special considerations for transition metals, and the concept of isoelectronic species.

core_concept.principle: Electron configurations for ions are written by adding electrons for anions (negative ions) or removing electrons for cations (positive ions) from the neutral atom configuration, with transition metals losing electrons from the highest principal quantum number ($n$) first.
core_concept.explanation: When atoms form ions, they gain or lose electrons to achieve more stable electron configurations, often matching noble gas configurations. For main group elements, electrons are added to or removed from the outermost shell. For transition metals, electrons are removed from the $s$ orbital of the highest $n$ before the $d$ orbitals (e.g., Fe loses 4s electrons before 3d). Anions have more electrons than the neutral atom; cations have fewer. Isoelectronic species have the same electron configuration but different nuclear charges.

image.alt: Diagram showing neutral atom vs. cation vs. anion electron configurations with electron additions and removals indicated
image.caption: Cations lose electrons (usually from highest $n$); anions gain electrons (added to outermost subshell)

sections.introduction: Anions are negatively charged ions formed when atoms gain electrons. Nonmetals typically form anions by gaining electrons to achieve noble gas electron configurations, making them more stable. Writing anion configurations requires adding electrons to the neutral atom configuration.

key_concept.definition: Anions are formed by adding electrons to the neutral atom configuration; the number of electrons added equals the magnitude of the negative charge.
key_concept.context: Anions typically form from nonmetals in Groups 15-17, which gain electrons to complete their valence shells and achieve noble gas configurations.

principles.statement: For anions, add electrons equal to the negative charge to the neutral atom configuration, placing them in the outermost available subshell.
principles.application: Determines the electron configuration of any anion; added electrons go to the lowest energy available orbital.
principles.analogy: Like filling seats in a theater: new people (electrons) fill the available seats (orbitals) in the current row (subshell).

principles.statement: Most anions achieve noble gas electron configurations by gaining electrons to fill their valence shells.
principles.application: Explains why anions are stable; noble gas configurations have complete subshells with low energy.
principles.example: F⁻: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (same as Ne); Cl⁻: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6$ (same as Ar)
principles.analogy: Like completing a puzzle: anions gain electrons to complete their valence shell 'puzzle'.

principles.statement: Anions are often isoelectronic with noble gases, having the same number of electrons and electron configuration.
principles.application: Enables prediction of anion properties based on noble gas analogs; explains anion stability.
principles.example: O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺ all have 10 electrons with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (isoelectronic with Ne)
principles.analogy: Like different people wearing the same uniform: different identities (nuclear charges) but same appearance (electron configuration).

classifications.value: Gain 3 electrons to achieve noble gas configuration
classifications.behavior: Formed by nitrogen family elements; achieve $ns^2 np^6$ configuration

examples.explanation: N: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^3$; N³⁻: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (isoelectronic with Ne)
P: [Ne] $3s^2 3p^3$; P³⁻: [Ne] $3s^2 3p^6$ = [Ar] (isoelectronic with Ar)

classifications.value: Gain 2 electrons to achieve noble gas configuration
classifications.behavior: Formed by oxygen family elements; achieve $ns^2 np^6$ configuration

examples.explanation: O: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^4$; O²⁻: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (isoelectronic with Ne)
S: [Ne] $3s^2 3p^4$; S²⁻: [Ne] $3s^2 3p^6$ = [Ar] (isoelectronic with Ar)

classifications.value: Gain 1 electron to achieve noble gas configuration
classifications.behavior: Formed by halogens; achieve $ns^2 np^6$ configuration; most stable anions

examples.explanation: F: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^5$; F⁻: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (isoelectronic with Ne)
Cl: [Ne] $3s^2 3p^5$; Cl⁻: [Ne] $3s^2 3p^6$ = [Ar] (isoelectronic with Ar)

worked_examples.title: Writing Configuration for Oxide Ion
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for the oxide ion (O²⁻).

step_by_step.explanation: Oxygen has Z = 8, so neutral O has 8 electrons
O²⁻ has 2- charge, meaning 2 extra electrons added
O²⁻ has same configuration as Ne (10 electrons)

worked_examples.concept_applied: For anions, add electrons equal to negative charge to the neutral atom configuration; add to outermost subshell.

worked_examples.title: Writing Configuration for Chloride Ion
worked_examples.problem: Write the noble gas notation for the chloride ion (Cl⁻).

step_by_step.explanation: Chlorine has Z = 17, so neutral Cl has 17 electrons
Cl⁻ has 1- charge, meaning 1 extra electron added
Cl⁻ has same configuration as Ar (18 electrons)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Halide ions achieve noble gas configurations by gaining one electron to fill p subshell.

sections.key_concept: Cations are positively charged ions formed when atoms lose electrons. Metals typically form cations by losing electrons from their outermost shell. For transition metals, electrons are removed from the $s$ orbital of the highest principal quantum number before the $d$ orbitals.

categories.description: Main group metals lose electrons from their outermost $s$ and $p$ orbitals to achieve noble gas configurations.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Electrons are removed from the highest energy occupied orbitals, which for main group elements are the outermost $s$ and $p$ orbitals.

examples.explanation: Na: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$; Na⁺: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (lose 3s electron; isoelectronic with Ne)
Mg: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2$; Mg²⁺: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (lose both 3s electrons; isoelectronic with Ne)
Al: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^1$; Al³⁺: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (lose 3s and 3p electrons; isoelectronic with Ne)

categories.description: Transition metals lose electrons from the $s$ orbital of highest $n$ before losing $d$ electrons, contrary to filling order.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Although 4s fills before 3d, when forming cations, 4s electrons are removed first because they are in a higher principal energy level and are farther from the nucleus on average.

examples.explanation: Fe: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^6$; Fe²⁺: [Ar] $3d^6$ (lose both 4s electrons, NOT 3d electrons)
Fe: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^6$; Fe³⁺: [Ar] $3d^5$ (lose 4s² and one 3d electron)
Cu: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$; Cu²⁺: [Ar] $3d^9$ (lose 4s¹ and one 3d electron)

categories.description: Many transition metals can form cations with different charges, resulting in different electron configurations.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Different oxidation states remove different numbers of electrons, always from highest $n$ first, then from $d$ orbitals.

examples.explanation: Cr²⁺: [Ar] $3d^4$; Cr³⁺: [Ar] $3d^3$ (progressive removal from 4s then 3d)
Mn²⁺: [Ar] $3d^5$; Mn³⁺: [Ar] $3d^4$; Mn⁴⁺: [Ar] $3d^3$ (half-filled $d^5$ is particularly stable)

classifications.value: Lose 1 electron from $ns^1$ to achieve noble gas configuration
classifications.behavior: Formed by alkali metals; achieve $ns^2 np^6$ configuration of previous noble gas

examples.explanation: Na: [Ne] $3s^1$; Na⁺: [Ne] (lose 3s electron; isoelectronic with Ne)
K: [Ar] $4s^1$; K⁺: [Ar] (lose 4s electron; isoelectronic with Ar)

classifications.value: Lose 2 electrons from $ns^2$ to achieve noble gas configuration
classifications.behavior: Formed by alkaline earth metals; achieve $ns^2 np^6$ configuration of previous noble gas

examples.explanation: Mg: [Ne] $3s^2$; Mg²⁺: [Ne] (lose both 3s electrons; isoelectronic with Ne)
Ca: [Ar] $4s^2$; Ca²⁺: [Ar] (lose both 4s electrons; isoelectronic with Ar)

classifications.value: Lose $s$ electrons before $d$ electrons; multiple oxidation states common
classifications.behavior: Electrons removed from $s$ orbital of highest $n$ first, then from $d$ orbitals

examples.explanation: Fe: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^6$; Fe²⁺: [Ar] $3d^6$ (lose 4s² only)
Fe: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^6$; Fe³⁺: [Ar] $3d^5$ (lose 4s² and one 3d)

worked_examples.title: Writing Configuration for Sodium Ion
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for the sodium ion (Na⁺).

step_by_step.explanation: Sodium has Z = 11, so neutral Na has 11 electrons
Na⁺ has 1+ charge, meaning 1 electron removed
Na⁺ has same configuration as Ne (10 electrons)

worked_examples.concept_applied: For main group cations, remove electrons from outermost subshell (highest $n$).

worked_examples.title: Writing Configuration for Iron(III) Ion
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for the iron(III) ion (Fe³⁺).

step_by_step.explanation: Iron has Z = 26, so neutral Fe has 26 electrons
Fe³⁺ has 3+ charge, meaning 3 electrons removed

step_by_step.calculation: Fe³⁺ more stable than Fe²⁺ in many compounds

worked_examples.final_answer: Iron(III) ion (Fe³⁺): [Ar] $3d^5$ (half-filled d subshell, particularly stable)
worked_examples.concept_applied: For transition metal cations, remove electrons from $s$ orbital of highest $n$ first, then from $d$ orbitals.

sections.section_title: Special Rule: Transition Metals Lose $s$ Electrons First
sections.key_concept: A critical rule for transition metal cations: although $s$ orbitals fill before $d$ orbitals in neutral atoms (4s before 3d), electrons are removed from the $s$ orbital of the highest principal quantum number first when forming cations.

components.definition: For transition metals, when forming cations, electrons are removed from the $s$ orbital with the highest $n$ value before removing any $d$ electrons, even though $s$ orbitals fill before $d$ orbitals in neutral atoms.
components.interpretation: This occurs because 4s electrons are farther from the nucleus on average and are less tightly held than 3d electrons once the atom is ionized.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron removal from 4s before 3d for transition metal cations
image.caption: Transition metals lose 4s electrons before 3d electrons when forming cations

principles.statement: When forming cations, electrons are always removed from orbitals with the highest principal quantum number ($n$) first.
principles.application: Explains why 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons for first-row transition metals.
principles.analogy: Like removing outer layers of clothing first: outermost electrons (highest $n$) come off before inner electrons.

principles.statement: The relative energies of 4s and 3d orbitals change upon ionization; 3d becomes lower in energy than 4s in cations.
principles.application: Explains why 4s electrons are removed first despite filling first in neutral atoms.
principles.example: Neutral Fe: 4s < 3d in energy; Fe²⁺: 3d < 4s in energy
principles.analogy: Like a building where the top floor becomes less stable than lower floors once some residents leave.

principles.statement: Half-filled ($d^5$) and fully-filled ($d^{10}$) $d$ subshells provide extra stability, influencing which electrons are removed.
principles.application: Explains why Fe³⁺ ($d^5$) is often more stable than Fe²⁺ ($d^6$) and why Cu⁺ ($d^{10}$) is stable.
principles.analogy: Like a symmetrical arrangement being more stable than an asymmetrical one.

worked_examples.title: Why 4s Electrons Are Removed Before 3d
worked_examples.problem: Explain why iron loses 4s electrons before 3d electrons when forming Fe²⁺, even though 4s fills before 3d in the neutral atom.

step_by_step.step: Recall filling order for neutral atoms
step_by_step.explanation: In neutral atoms, 4s has lower energy than 3d, so 4s fills first
When electrons are removed, the effective nuclear charge increases for remaining electrons
In cations, 3d orbitals become lower in energy than 4s due to better penetration and less shielding
Electrons are removed from highest $n$ first; 4s has $n=4$, 3d has $n=3$
Filling order ≠ removal order; removal follows highest $n$ first

worked_examples.final_answer: 4s electrons are removed before 3d because 4s has higher principal quantum number ($n=4$ vs. $n=3$) and becomes higher in energy than 3d upon ionization
worked_examples.concept_applied: Electron removal order follows highest $n$ first, not filling order; 4s removed before 3d for transition metals.

worked_examples.title: Writing Configuration for Copper(II) Ion
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for Cu²⁺, given that neutral Cu is [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Cu²⁺ has one unpaired electron in 3d; paramagnetic

worked_examples.concept_applied: For transition metals with exceptions (Cu, Cr), still remove from highest $n$ ($s$ orbital) first, then from $d$ orbitals.

sections.key_concept: Isoelectronic species are atoms or ions that have the same number of electrons and the same electron configuration, but different nuclear charges. Understanding isoelectronic series helps predict properties and stability of ions.

process_flow.description: For each species, calculate total electrons: neutral = Z; anion = Z + charge; cation = Z - charge.
process_flow.result: Total electron count for each species

process_flow.description: Species with the same total electron count are potentially isoelectronic.
process_flow.result: Identify species with matching electron counts

process_flow.description: Write electron configurations to confirm they are identical.
Different nuclear charges (Z) mean different ionic radii and properties despite same configuration.
process_flow.result: Understanding of property trends in isoelectronic series

process_flow.description: Higher nuclear charge in isoelectronic series → smaller radius, higher ionization energy.
process_flow.result: Property predictions based on nuclear charge

classifications.behavior: Increasing nuclear charge decreases ionic radius in isoelectronic series

examples.explanation: H⁻ > He > Li⁺ > Be²⁺ (radius decreases as nuclear charge increases)

characteristics: N³⁻, O²⁻, F⁻, Ne, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺
classifications.behavior: Anions larger than neutral, cations smaller; radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge

examples.explanation: N³⁻ > O²⁻ > F⁻ > Ne > Na⁺ > Mg²⁺ > Al³⁺ (radius decreases left to right)
All achieve stable noble gas configuration; common in nature

characteristics: P³⁻, S²⁻, Cl⁻, Ar, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Sc³⁺
classifications.behavior: Same electron configuration but different chemical properties due to different nuclear charges

examples.explanation: Cl⁻ is stable anion; K⁺ is stable cation; Ar is inert gas; all have same configuration

classifications.value: Transition metal ions can be isoelectronic with each other
classifications.behavior: Similar magnetic and spectroscopic properties within isoelectronic transition metal series

examples.explanation: Mn²⁺ and Fe³⁺ both have 5 unpaired $d$ electrons; both strongly paramagnetic

worked_examples.title: Identifying Isoelectronic Species
worked_examples.problem: Which of the following are isoelectronic with Ne (10 electrons): O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺?

step_by_step.explanation: Ne has Z = 10, neutral, so 10 electrons
O has Z = 8; O²⁻ has 8 + 2 = 10 electrons
F has Z = 9; F⁻ has 9 + 1 = 10 electrons
Na has Z = 11; Na⁺ has 11 - 1 = 10 electrons
Mg has Z = 12; Mg²⁺ has 12 - 2 = 10 electrons
Al has Z = 13; Al³⁺ has 13 - 3 = 10 electrons
All listed species have 10 electrons and are isoelectronic with Ne
step_by_step.calculation: All are isoelectronic: O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, Ne

worked_examples.final_answer: All species (O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺) are isoelectronic with Ne; all have 10 electrons with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Isoelectronic species have same number of electrons and same electron configuration but different nuclear charges.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Ionic Radii in Isoelectronic Series
worked_examples.problem: Arrange the following isoelectronic ions in order of increasing ionic radius: N³⁻, O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺.

step_by_step.explanation: For isoelectronic species, higher nuclear charge pulls electrons closer, decreasing radius
Radius increases as nuclear charge decreases
step_by_step.calculation: Al³⁺ < Mg²⁺ < Na⁺ < F⁻ < O²⁻ < N³⁻

step_by_step.explanation: In isoelectronic series, radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge
step_by_step.calculation: Anions > neutral > cations (for same electron count)

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing ionic radius: Al³⁺ < Mg²⁺ < Na⁺ < F⁻ < O²⁻ < N³⁻ (radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge in isoelectronic series)
worked_examples.concept_applied: In isoelectronic series, ionic radius is inversely related to nuclear charge; higher Z = smaller radius.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about ion electron configurations can lead to incorrect predictions about ion properties and reactivity. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding of ion formation.

classifications.type: Error: Removing $d$ Electrons Before $s$ for Transition Metals
classifications.value: Removing electrons from $d$ orbitals before $s$ orbitals when forming transition metal cations
characteristics: Following filling order instead of removal order
classifications.behavior: Remember: remove from highest $n$ first ($s$ before $d$ for transition metals)

examples.explanation: Wrong: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$; Correct: [Ar] $3d^6$ (remove 4s before 3d)
Wrong: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^3$; Correct: [Ar] $3d^5$ (remove 4s before 3d)

classifications.type: Error: Incorrect Electron Count for Ions
classifications.value: Not adjusting electron count correctly for ion charge
characteristics: Forgetting to add electrons for anions
Forgetting to remove electrons for cations
Adding instead of removing for cations
classifications.behavior: Anions: add electrons equal to negative charge; Cations: remove electrons equal to positive charge

examples.explanation: Wrong: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^4$ (neutral O); Correct: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (added 2e⁻)
Wrong: [Ar] $4s^2$ (neutral Ca); Correct: [Ar] (removed 2e⁻)

classifications.value: Assuming isoelectronic species have identical properties
classifications.behavior: Isoelectronic means same electron configuration, NOT same properties; nuclear charge affects radius, ionization energy, etc.

examples.explanation: Both isoelectronic with Ne, but Na⁺ is cation (smaller), F⁻ is anion (larger); different chemical behavior

classifications.type: Error: Assuming All Ions Achieve Noble Gas Configurations
classifications.value: Thinking all ions have noble gas electron configurations
characteristics: Assuming transition metal ions are isoelectronic with noble gases
classifications.behavior: Main group ions typically achieve noble gas configurations; transition metal ions typically do not (retain $d$ electrons)

examples.explanation: Wrong: assumes noble gas configuration; Correct: [Ar] $3d^6$ (not noble gas configuration)
Wrong: assumes noble gas configuration; Correct: [Ar] $3d^9$ (not noble gas configuration)

classifications.type: Error: Incorrect Noble Gas Core for Ions
classifications.value: Using wrong noble gas core when writing abbreviated configurations for ions
characteristics: Using noble gas from same period instead of previous
Confusing ion configuration with neutral atom
classifications.behavior: Use the noble gas that represents the core electrons after ionization; for cations, this may be the previous noble gas

examples.explanation: Wrong: [Ar] $4s^0$; Correct: [Ar] (K⁺ is isoelectronic with Ar)
Wrong: [Ar] $4s^0$; Correct: [Ar] (Ca²⁺ is isoelectronic with Ar)

sections.section_title: Practice Problems: Electron Configurations of Ions
sections.introduction: Test your understanding of ion electron configurations with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces understanding of electron addition/removal for ions, transition metal cation rules, and isoelectronic species.
key_concept.context: Focus on correct electron counts, proper removal order for transition metals, and isoelectronic relationships.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for the sulfide ion (S²⁻).

step_by_step.explanation: Sulfur has Z = 16, so neutral S has 16 electrons
S²⁻ has 2- charge, meaning 2 extra electrons added
Add 2 electrons to neutral S configuration ([Ne] $3s^2 3p^4$)

worked_examples.concept_applied: For anions, add electrons equal to negative charge to achieve noble gas configuration.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for the zinc ion (Zn²⁺).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Transition metals lose $s$ electrons before $d$ electrons; Zn²⁺ retains filled $d^{10}$.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Which of the following are isoelectronic with Ar (18 electrons): K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, S²⁻, Sc³⁺?
worked_examples.final_answer: All species (K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻, S²⁻, Sc³⁺) are isoelectronic with Ar; all have 18 electrons
worked_examples.concept_applied: Isoelectronic species have same electron count; verify by calculating Z ± charge.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Explain why Cr³⁺ has the configuration [Ar] $3d^3$ rather than [Ar] $4s^2 3d^1$.

step_by_step.explanation: Transition metals always lose $s$ electrons before $d$ electrons

worked_examples.final_answer: Cr³⁺ is [Ar] $3d^3$ because transition metals lose $s$ electrons (highest $n$) before $d$ electrons; remove 4s¹ then 2 from 3d⁵
worked_examples.concept_applied: Transition metal cation rule: remove $s$ electrons before $d$ electrons, regardless of filling order.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Arrange the following isoelectronic ions in order of decreasing ionic radius: O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺.

step_by_step.explanation: In isoelectronic series, radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge
Lowest Z = largest radius; highest Z = smallest radius

worked_examples.final_answer: Decreasing ionic radius: O²⁻ > F⁻ > Na⁺ > Mg²⁺ (radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge in isoelectronic series)
worked_examples.concept_applied: In isoelectronic series, ionic radius is inversely proportional to nuclear charge.

summary: Electron configurations for ions are written by adjusting the neutral atom configuration: anions gain electrons (add to outermost subshell), cations lose electrons (remove from highest $n$ first). For main group elements, ions typically achieve noble gas configurations (O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺ all isoelectronic with Ne). For transition metals, electrons are removed from $s$ orbital of highest $n$ before $d$ orbitals (Fe: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^6$ → Fe²⁺: [Ar] $3d^6$ → Fe³⁺: [Ar] $3d^5$), contrary to filling order. Isoelectronic species have same electron count and configuration but different nuclear charges (N³⁻, O²⁻, F⁻, Ne, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺ all have 10 electrons); in isoelectronic series, ionic radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge. Common errors include: removing $d$ electrons before $s$ for transition metals, incorrect electron counts for ions, assuming all ions achieve noble gas configurations (transition metals typically don't), and confusing isoelectronic with identical properties. Key rules: (1) anions add electrons to outermost subshell; (2) main group cations remove from outermost shell; (3) transition metal cations remove $s$ before $d$; (4) verify electron count = Z ± charge. Mastery enables accurate prediction of ion configurations, magnetic properties, ionic radii trends, and chemical behavior of ions in compounds.
